author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: If the concentration is too high it will agglomerate. You can just see this happen.

author: @energeticallybored
text: I really enjoy these videos.  I am wondering how you determine if the final form is too concentrated so it requires the additional polyethylene glycol and isopropanol alcohol.  It is based on how opaque the colloidal dispersion is?  If so, are you just eyeballing it, and going from experience,or is there a reference chart or measurement that can be done?  Or is it simply that the final product isn't coming out as well as expected from a test batch?

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: maintains the colloidal nature of the graphene - it's to do with the surface charges on the graphene sheet. But again - I liked your question.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Good question and it is something I should have mentioned on the video but not everything occurs to me at the time - which is why i like the comments pages. Anyway, It is not actually a solution but a colloidal dispersion (you can Tyndall test it for this) and if you reduce it in too concentrated a form the graphene restacks to graphite almost immediately unless you add a dispersant - I use 1% polyethylene glycol and 1% isopropanol alcohol for this sometimes - dilute and in pH balance maintains

author: @MattSyTy
text:
I appreciate the videos here, but I'm going to try to obtain few layer graphene by sonication.  
The abstract on doing this using a surfactant like acacia gum is available on the net and should be of reproducible for me.  Sonication > Centrifugation > Acid Reduction > freeze drying.  
I bought about 800 grams from your friends mine along with some diamagnetic graphite.  I have everything assembled except for the freeze dryer.  This project is one of my more expensive endeavors.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Vacuum filter a well dispersed solution then heat to 200 degrees centigrade for an hour. Flow direction filtering is best.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Absolutely. There are quite a few ways of doing it to make thin films, thick film and sponges with some very interesting properties
